item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really sweet. My only gripe is with how repetitive the "characteristic" logs are, which makes the centrepiece of this work a slog, but altogether this is an enjoyable and well-written skip. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright I don't understand this because of my basic thought process its way too confusing. Can anyone explain it? Also shouldn't this be marked as "Keter" (or possibly Apollyon) because it is very hard / impossible to contain?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So, it has been mentioned up thread that this article shares many themes with SCP-1342, so much so that I'd argue that a link between the two would not go amiss. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

On 12/14/2010, a high frequency (400 GHz) radio signal was detected originating from the region of space that SCP-2499 is located in. The signal itself broadcasted the entirety of a recording of Gustav Holst’s 7 movement orchestral suite, The Planets. Notably, the recording played was the 1926 recording of the piece, and the first one that was available for radio broadcast. Following this transmission, a second transmission, consisting entirely of a series of binary numbers was played. When the numbers were translated into English, a discernible message was revealed. It has been transcribed below.

To this:

On 12/14/2010, a high frequency (400 GHz) radio signal was detected originating from the region of space that SCP-2499 is located in. The signal itself broadcasted the entirety of a recording of Gustav Holst’s 7 movement orchestral suite, The Planets. Notably, the recording played was the 1926 recording of the piece, and the first one that was available for radio broadcast. Following this transmission, a second transmission, consisting entirely of a series of binary numbers was played. When the numbers were translated into English, a discernible message was revealed. It has been transcribed below.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: been said before
item.content:encoded:
It is rare that I simply just read an esceepee and smile.
The balancing act here is an Asimov level of assured writing.

It wasn't an overstated "doom, doom, with a side order of doom, a doom flavoured coffee to follow and a side of doom mints by the bill of doom" that some esceepees seem to aim for. Nor was it "oh I made a funny, look at the funny, isn't it funny why are you not laughing at the funny? Come on laugh at the funny I made, please.. now" some joke esceepees seem to aspire to. Or even the "I am a VERY serious example of an SCP tale - don't look for the humour, it is not there, you will never find it" mindset that appears in some instances.

No, this one works on a simple, quirky little tale that is just charming. Avoiding all the other directions it could have gone as outlined above.

Dancing stars - what is not to like?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Stars liked the music, so they started dancing

Love it. Hits the conventional "why does their FTL only work in one direction" question, but I never dwell on that, haha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Movement of SCP-2499-3 through SCP-2499-14 maintain the circle of the prior stage, while SCP-2499-1 and SCP-2499-2 are noted to move to the center of the circle … SCP-2499-1 and SCP-2499-2 move at 160 km/s.

If it's moving at 0.05% the speed of light, yet can get to center of the formation in about a decade a so, the stars must be really close together. Could non-anomalous telescopes be able to tell distinguish them all when they're that far away?

Following this transmission, a second transmission, consisting entirely of a series of binary numbers was played. When the numbers were translated into English,

Were they ASCII? Or 1 = A and 26 = Z?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was pleasantly mystified until the last section, and then that just killed it for me. It's far too telly for me - though there's no way I would have got it without. I think it needs more subtlety of explanation, but not too much… also, you can't just translate binary to text. Binary is just one number unless you have something clearly separating it up, and if you want text, some encoding method (the commonest of which is Unicode, though not invented until well after 1926).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Star aliens?

Dancing star aliens?

DANCING STAR ALIENS WITH A PENCHANT FOR CLASSICAL MUSIC?

YES! YES! YES!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fundamentally, it's a sentient thing that shouldn't be sentient, which is a bit cliche. I was all ready to feel apathetic towards this article, but then that last note made me feel happy. Curse you for ruining my cool demeanor, Weizhong!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Derp. My bad. Fixing now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has been brought up before but I noticed it too and it hasn't been fixed yet:
5.89 is less bright than 5.1, right?
But you have 5.1 listed as the lowest luminosity and 5.89 as the 2nd lowest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this. The translated message at the end is what sealed the upvote for me. Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was walking home from swimming last night and I saw the stars arrayed across the ebony canvas of the heavens twinkling downwards at me. One star arced and streaked across my view moving so gracefully.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Replace the song with "Straight Outta Compton" by NWA. Does the article have the same impact?

No.
Here's why

music is highly subjective. You chose this piece of music to have some significance to stars. I've never heard this song before. I may not even like it. I might find it to be bland, boring, or otherwise distasteful. When you throw in trying to pinpoint a specific music piece as having some other-worldly beauty, you risk alienating large portions of your potential audience.

That is the major reason I downvoted. The other being that it's just too similar to "To the Makers of Music". Kalinin put it succinctly if a bit harshly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love how it all ties together. The note itself is a bit unnerving; there are 14 sentient stars that appreciate good music about space. I shudder to think what their reaction would be to something like this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the sillier parts of the Hateful Star melded with a completely superficial imitation of the emotional impact from To The Makers of Music. The "infiltrate every single observatory in the whole world" containment procedures are beyond ridiculous, as is indeed any notion of containment, since at the apparent magnitudes of the stars in question, this is visible with the naked eye. You also use "luminosity" and "apparent magnitude" interchangeably, when they're not the same thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dancing with the stars? I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just noting this somewhere more permanent than chat BECAUSE THIS NEEDS TO BE WRITTEN. I DON'T CARE IF I WRITE IT OR SOMEBODY ELSE DOES. THIS NEEDS TO HAPPEN

First we had the Hateful Star, but this is the Cultured Star Cotillion, to which the Hateful Star was not in fact invited, because SCP-2499-1's parents think he's a beatnik. Instead, they set her up with the dreamy SCP-2499-2. Unfortunately, he's conceited, dry as a stick, and has an ass so tight he probably has clamps in his bathroom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final note is cute as hell, I really like it as the cause for what's going on, but I have to say I found the article itself dry and hard to follow. Guess it's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly. Thats why those numbers must be switched, because if you say the lowest is 5.1 and the second lowest (means brighter that previous) is 5.86

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very happy that you've achieved the success you deserved for this skip. Well done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very touching. The skip truly was a charming read and I love the idea and execution of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apparent magnitude luminosity actually means a decrease in luminosity the higher the values get.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We're using orbital velocity. Technically, it's not the correct unit since these things defy normal orbits, but I kept it because I felt that it was better using an established measurement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so cool!
I love the language that you use in this.
+1, my friend!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. I got it just before reading the note.

Little correction: you state that the second lowest luminosity state is 5.86 and the lowest is 5.1
These numbers must be switched, I guess, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Little quibble: in the description for the various stages, from what reference point are we basing the km/s measurement on?
I'm not actually into astronomy, but shouldn't it be something like degrees moved around the earth per sec?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this is really cool. I liked it in chat, and I like it now. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You already know that when I read this in chat, I didn't really get it. Now that I read it again, I just can't believe that I missed it. This article is clear while maintaining subtly, interesting, and beautifully written. Upvoted enthusiastically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nicely done with this one, was just good enough to earn my upvote. But despite the upvote, I just gotta say that with the physics-defying movements that the stars were making, I thought the entire thing was going to be like some sort of alien time-travelling device or somethin. Don't get me wrong, the giant dancing space dwellers are a nice idea, but something like an alien time machine woulda been better!

P.S.: I can't help but share this, but partially what made me upvote this was because this was a lot like a dream I once had. In the dream I was driving down a straight road in the night, when suddenly I noticed the sky. It was filled with giant planets and distant stars just whizzing around erratically in the night. I don't know why, anyone would have found this a great spectacle, but I found just plain frightening. Things like stars just shouldn't move that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh shit, I didn't +1? Must've forgot. Here you go. Making the note subtler would add on to the article's quality, but I don't really think it'll make much of a difference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I made the note a little less obvious, would it earn your upvote?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my God, dancing stars. This is going to be ridiculed for being "too silly" or something, but I don't care. This is awesome. Truthfully, I'd already sort of figured out that they were dancing before the last note, and I think it'd be better if you made the note a little less obvious. Other than that, jolly good show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Almost down voted until that note. It all clicked at that point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
YES

YES

YES

item.title: Author Overview
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to CryogenChaos, Devereaux, TwistedGears, EdAWACSdenyY, HatMan, and the other members of chat for looking at this.

For people who don’t get it:
SPOILERS

The stars themselves (or should I say “stars?”) are dancing to the music of The Planets. Each stage of the pulsating period for the SCP matches the various movements of the suite itself.

For example, the first movement of the suite, entitled “Mars - The Bringer of War” is a big, dramatic, intense, and fast piece that is full of pomp and grand order, much like a call to arms. Therefore, the movement of the stars matches that in its order, speed, and intensity of motion.

A good basis is, tempo=velocity, style=movement patterns, intensity=luminosity. There are other connections as well, but telling all of those would spoil the fun. :)
